Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Resonses To Special Interrogatories Adn Requests For Production Of Documents; Supporting Memo P/A
Off Calendar Apr-21-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances: Adrian Sawyer appeared by zoom for Sharon Browne Nassiri (Petitioner).
Joseph Barton appeared by zoom for Rosie Thompson (Respondent).
Court Reporter Raquel Sharp, CSR #10619, 925-384-0003, raquel@transcriptlegal.com

Counsel presented oral argument to support their positions in response to the Court's tentative ruling regarding special interrogatory 51 and 55, the issue of privacy interests in the settlement agreement, whether or not there was a trust currently in existence, the source of the settlement funds, etc. Upon conclusion of argument, with respect to special interrogatory 51, the Court permits Mr. Barton to orally amend his response and object to special interrogatory 51, stating that it would be unfair to default Mr. Barton given the objections otherwise asserted against the other Special Interrogatories at issue in this motion seeking production of the terms of the confidential settlement agreement between Respondent and Jane Browne Sanchez, as well as the specific objection to the request for production of the settlement document itself. The Court declines to expand its ruling on what should be further compelled. It finds sufficient support was provided regarding discovery of the source of the funds, but it is not moved by the oral advocacy regarding the other special interrogatories.
Petitioner Sharon Browne Nassiri's motion to compel further responses to Special Interrogatories numbers 51, 52-54, 55, and 56-57 is GRANTED IN PART and DENIED IN PART. Petitioner's motion to compel further response to Request for Production (Set Three) number 75 is DENIED.
The motion to compel further responses to Special Interrogatories 51 and 55 are denied as procedurally defective. Special Interrogatory 51 is not included in the Separate Statement, and Special Interrogatory number 55 is not listed in the Notice of Motion.
The motion to compel further response to Special Interrogatories numbers 52, 53, 56 and 57 is denied on the merits. The motion to compel further response to Special Interrogatory number 54 - the source of the settlement funds - is granted.
Respondent Rosie M. Thompson's evidentiary objections to parts of the declaration filed in support of Petitioner's Reply are overruled.
The Court will issue an order confirming its tentative ruling.

Judge: Ross C. Moody. Clerk: Rosie Noguera. Deputy: J Carranza. Not reported.

Notice Of Motion And Motion Of Petitioners To Require Trustee To Post Bond
Off Calendar Jul-22-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
John J. Stein, Esq. appearing by CourtCall for Jane Browne Sanchez and Sharon Browne Nassiri.
Joseph Barton, Esq. appearing by CourtCall for Rosie M. Thompson.
Francis X. Browne appearing by CourtCall as self-represented litigant.

Matter called, argument is held. At the conclusion of argument, the tentative ruling is adopted as follows:

Notwithstanding that the current existence of the Portside 503 Trust is disputed in this matter and will not be determined on this motion, Petitioners have alleged that they have a present interest as beneficiaries of the Portside 503 Trust to a distribution of $100,000 each, upon their father's death, and are otherwise contingent beneficiaries. Respondent has not disputed in the motion Petitioners' assertion that the Portside 503 Trust existed at some point in time, or that the Petitioners were entitled to such a distribution upon Decedent's death. The parties do not otherwise appear to dispute in the motion papers that (1) under the Portside 503 Trust, Petitioners were contingent beneficiaries, due to receive whatever remained of the Trust corpus at Respondent's death, and (2) Respondent was not restricted from consuming the entirety of the Trust corpus during her lifetime following Decedent's death, minus the initial $100,000 distribution to each of the Petitioners. Thus, the only non-contingent interests Petitioners have shown in this motion which necessitate protection pursuant to Probate Code 15602(a)(2) are their interests in the initial $100,000 distributions.

Respondent is ordered to post bond of $200,000, plus costs of bond. The cost of the bond shall be charged against the trust.

Respondent's opposition to the motion was untimely. Although Petitioners were able to file a substantive reply within the required period, going forward, the parties should anticipate that the court will require compliance with the relevant rules and code provisions.

The Court set status on bond for August 5, 2020 at 9:00 a.m., in Dept. 204.

Judge: Ross C. Moody. Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda. No bailiff. Matter not reported.

Notice Of Motion To Compel Further Responses To Written Discovery Set 2
Off Calendar Jul-22-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
John J. Stein, Esq. appearing by CourtCall for Jane Browne Sanchez and Sharon Browne Nassiri.
Joseph Barton, Esq. appearing by CourtCall for Rosie M. Thompson.
Francis X. Browne appearing as self-represented litigant.

Matter called, argument is held. At the conclusion of argument, the tentative ruling is adopted as follows:

The Motion to Compel Further Responses to Written Discovery Set 2 - Sharon Browne Nassiri is granted in part and denied in part.

While the Court agrees with Petitioner's time calculation of when this motion was due to be noticed, there is no showing that the responses at issue were verified and code-compliant under CCP sections 2030.299 and 2030.300, respectively. Both statutes calculate notice of the motion from service of verified responses. Sexton v. Superior Court (1997) 58 Cal. App. 4th 1403 relied on prior statutes, which did not contain the "verified" language.
Respondent is not correct that Article 1 section 28 abrogates Evidence Code 787 in civil cases. Under section 28(f), "Any prior felony conviction of any person in any criminal proceeding, whether adult or juvenile, shall subsequently be used without limitation for purposes of impeachment or enhancement of sentence in any criminal proceeding [....]." People v. Dalton (2019) 7 Cal. 5th 166, 213-14 analyzed section 28's effect within a criminal case context. Section 28(f) does not apply to civil cases (Robbins v. Wong (1994) 27 Cal.App.4th 261, 274). A witness' credibility may be impeached with evidence of a prior felony conviction in a civil case, subject to the court's powers under Evidence Code section 352.

However, admissibility at trial is not the test of whether discovery is proper. Under CCP section 2017.010, "[A]ny party may obtain discovery regarding any matter, not privileged, that is relevant to the subject matter involved in the pending action or to the determination of any motion made in that action, if the matter either is itself admissible in evidence or appears reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. Discovery may relate to the claim or defense of the party seeking discovery or of any other party to the action."

The motion to compel further responses to discovery is granted as to the disputed Requests for Admission and Form Interrogatories pertaining to Ms. Nassiri being charged with a felony, or entering a plea to a lesser felony charge, either of which may be reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence for the purposes of impeaching Ms. Nassiri's testimony as a witness at trial. The motion is denied as to the Requests for Admission and Form Interrogatories pertaining to locations and institutions in which Ms. Nassiri may have been detained. Discovery about federal charges or pleadings to federal charges may lead to relevant discovery about federal convictions, but where a person was held in custody does not appear reasonably calculated to lead to admissible impeachment evidence of a conviction.
The Court further notes that Ms. Nassiri acknowledges in her declaration in support of the opposition to this motion she may have been convicted of a felony in the 1970's, and seemed willing to attempt to further answer Form Interrogatory 2.8 after the relevant court records departments resumed operations sufficiently to allow such research, but Respondent denied her request for an extension of time to respond.

The parties are encouraged to further meet and confer in light of this tentative ruling.

The Court stated that responses are due in two weeks.

Judge: Ross C. Moody. Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda. No bailiff. Matter not reported

Petition: 1. To Confirm Existence Of Trust; 2. To Suspend And Remove Trustee; 3. To Appoint Trustee; 4. To Confirm Ownership Of Trust Property; 5. For Accounting; 6. To Instruct Trustee; And 7. For Breach Of Trust;
Off Calendar Sep-09-2019 Continued To Nov-04-19 At 09:00 A.M. In Dept 204
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances: David Baer for Jane Sanchez and Sharon Nassiri.
Joseph Barton for Rosie Thompson (Respondent).

Mr. Baer stated that there is a threshold issue that boils down to whether this trust was ever revoked. The Court noted that it can't say a trust exist unless it can identify the terms of a trust and that this can't be tried in three hours. Mr. Baer stated that if they can't prove the terms of the trust it's the Trustee's fault, and that if a trust is revoked the Trustee has a duty to inform the Beneficiaries and has the responsibility to carry out the terms of the trust. The Court noted that all of the issues are really intertwined and that it has to be tried all at once. Mr. Baer stated that there is no dispute at all that a trust was created, that Ms. Thompson filed a change in Trustee, that by her own admission a trust existed and that they want to be able to gain access to the Trustor's computer. The Parties are to meet and confer. The Court wants a discovery plan/schedule. The Petition to Confirm Existence of Trust, etc. is continued to November 4, 2019 at 9:00 a.m., in Dept. 204. A joint status report is to be provided by October 28, 2019. The meet and confer is to commence no later than October 1, 2019.

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn. Clerk: Rosie Noguera. Deputy: C. Kabasares. Not reported.

Joseph Barton for Rosie Thompson (Decedents' Widow).

The Court noted that the spousal property petition has now been dismissed, that there is a discovery hearing on calendar for August 22nd, and asked the Parties were they are and how they intend to go forward. Mr. Barton stated that he just received a supplemental status report yesterday, that he did not see anything in the notes with regard to their objections, that he believes the petition does not allege existence of the trust and that no copy is available. Mr. Barton also stated that there is a significant issue of standing that has not been addressed and that he will file a motion. Mr. Baer stated that he thinks the Court should suspend Ms. Thompson and find that the trust exist based on what's been submitted. The Court stated that it can't get there and noted that there is no motion for judgment on the pleadings before it. Mr. Baer presented oral argument to support suspension of Ms. Thompson and stated that there is significant evidence that the trust existed. Discussion was held with respect to setting a hearing on the issue of suspension and setting an evidentiary hearing on the issue of existence of the trust. The Petition to Confirm Existence of Trust, to Suspend and Remove Trustee, etc. is continued to September 9, 2019 at 9:00 a.m., in Dept. 204 to be heard by Judge Quinn and to allow the parties to file what they intend to file to get the issue of standing and existence of the trust properly before the Court. The Court suggests that the Parties talk to each other and try to resolve this.
